title: Input & Output
description: Reading user input, printing output, string formatting, and I/O internals — buffering, file descriptors, encoding pitfalls, and memory-mapped I/O. FAANG-depth coverage of Python's I/O stack.
details:
Python’s I/O stack has three layers: (1) raw file descriptors (os-level, unbuffered), (2) buffered streams (io.BufferedReader, io.BufferedWriter), and (3) text streams (io.TextIOWrapper with encoding/decoding). The built-in open() function abstracts all three layers based on the mode parameter.

Buffering matters. By default, text mode uses line buffering (buffering=1, flushes on newline), binary mode uses a buffer of io.DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE (8192 bytes). Set buffering=0 for unbuffered (writes go straight to the kernel — useful for logging crashes). Set buffering=-1 for the default. A large buffer reduces syscall overhead but increases memory and data loss risk on crash.

Encoding is the silent killer. Python 3 uses the system encoding (usually UTF-8) by default, but LANG=C or Windows can give ASCII/latin-1. Always pass encoding='utf-8' explicitly in production code. The errors parameter controls decoding failures: 'strict' (raise), 'replace' (�), 'backslashreplace' (\xNN), 'surrogateescape' (file system paths).

File descriptors & resource limits. Each open() consumes an OS file descriptor. ulimit -n caps the count (typically 256–unlimited). Leaking descriptors causes “Too many open files”. Always use context managers (with open(...) as f:) which call f.__exit__ → f.close() even on exceptions. For long-running servers, consider cloexec flags to prevent fd leaks across fork().

Memory-mapped I/O via mmap maps a file directly into virtual memory, avoiding syscall overhead for reads/writes. The OS manages paging transparently. This is useful for large files (GB+) where random access is needed — the kernel handles the on-demand page loads.

sections.title: Basic Output (print) — Buffers & Streams
sections.description: The print() function writes to sys.stdout, a text stream that wraps the underlying file descriptor 1. The file argument redirects to any object with a .write() method. For real-time output (log tailing, subprocess), flush explicitly: print(..., flush=True) or set sys.stdout.reconfigure(line_buffering=True). Without flushing, output may linger in user-space buffers.
sections.codeBlock:
# sys.stdout is a TextIOWrapper
import sys
print("Hello")  # shorthand for sys.stdout.write("Hello\n")

# Custom separator and end
print("a", "b", "c", sep=", ", end="!\n")  # a, b, c!

# Force flush for real-time output
print("Processing...", end="", flush=True)

# Redirect to file or stderr
with open("log.txt", "w") as f:
    print("[ERROR] crash", file=f)
print("WARN", file=sys.stderr)

# Check buffer size
print(sys.stdout.buffer)      # BufferedWriter
print(sys.stdout.buffer.raw)   # FileIO (raw fd)

# Unbuffered mode:
# sys.stdout.reconfigure(line_buffering=True)
# sys.stdout.reconfigure(write_through=True)  # no buffer at all

sections.title: User Input, Encoding & Edge Cases
sections.description: The input() function reads a line from sys.stdin, strips the trailing newline, and returns a string. All encoding happens under the hood via sys.stdin.encoding. If the input contains bytes that don’t match the terminal encoding, UnicodeDecodeError is raised. For binary input (piped data, images), read sys.stdin.buffer.read() directly. The input() call is blocking — for timeouts, use sys.stdin with select.select() or threading.
sections.codeBlock:
# Standard: returns str, strips trailing \n
name = input("Enter name: ")
age = int(input("Enter age: "))  # ValueError if non-numeric

# Secure: wrap in try/except
# try:
#     val = int(input())
# except ValueError:
#     print("Invalid number")

# Binary input (piped data, e.g. cat image.jpg | python script.py)
import sys
raw_bytes = sys.stdin.buffer.read()

# Non-blocking input check (Unix)
import sys, select
if select.select([sys.stdin], [], [], 0.1)[0]:
    line = sys.stdin.readline()
else:
    print("No input within timeout")

# Encoding pitfall:
# export LANG=C && echo "café" | python3 -c "print(input())"
# This FAILS — latin-1 encoding can't decode 'é'
# Fix: PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 python3 -c "print(input())"

sections.title: F-strings — Performance & Pitfalls
sections.description: F-strings (PEP 498) are the fastest formatting option in Python 3.6+. They compile to bytecode that constructs the string with BUILD_STRING opcode, avoiding method call overhead. Inside f-strings, arbitrary expressions are evaluated: f"{fn(x)}", f"{obj.attr}", even f"{x=}" (debug syntax, Python 3.8+). Security: never embed user-supplied template strings in f-strings — use .format() with safe templates or string.Template instead. F-string expressions are evaluated at runtime with full access to locals/globals — a SQL injection-like risk if the format string itself comes from user input.
sections.codeBlock:
name = "Alice"
age = 30

# Debug syntax (3.8+): prints name=value
print(f"{name=}, {age=}")  # name='Alice', age=30

# Expression evaluation
print(f"{2 * 3 + 1}")         # 7
print(f"{name.upper()}")      # ALICE

# Nested f-strings (for dynamic precision)
precision = 3
print(f"{3.14159:.{precision}f}")  # 3.142

# SECURITY: DO NOT do this with user input
# user_template = "{__import__('os').system('id')}"
# result = f"{user_template}"  # DANGEROUS — code execution!

# Safe approach: string.Template
from string import Template
t = Template("Hello $name")
print(t.safe_substitute(name="Bob"))  # ignores unknown vars, no runtime eval

sections.title: File I/O — Open Modes & Binary Safety
sections.description: The open() function uses different buffering layers: 'r' (text, buffered), 'rb' (binary, buffered), 'w' (truncate + write), 'a' (append, O_APPEND). The '+' modifier enables read+write. On Unix, 'a' mode uses O_APPEND — writes go to the EOF atomically even with multiple writers (kernel guarantees it). On Windows, 'b' mode avoids CRLF translation. Always close fds — leaking fds shows up as ResourceWarning in debug mode (python -Wd).
sections.codeBlock:
# Text mode (default): encoding matters
with open("data.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    content = f.read()

# Binary mode: no encoding, no \n translation
with open("image.jpg", "rb") as f:
    raw = f.read()

# Explicit buffer control
with open("large.bin", "wb", buffering=65536) as f:
    f.write(data)  # 64KB buffer reduces syscall count

# Unbuffered binary (use for crash-safe logging)
with open("emergency.log", "ab", buffering=0) as f:
    f.write(b"CRASH\n")  # immediately visible in tail -f

# File descriptor leak detection
# python -Wd script.py  # shows ResourceWarning on unclosed files

# os-level fd check:
# import os
# os.listdir(f'/proc/{os.getpid()}/fd')  # Linux
# lsof -p <pid>  # cross-platform

sections.title: Memory-Mapped I/O (mmap) — Zero-Copy Reads
sections.description: Memory-mapped files (mmap) map a file into virtual memory, letting you access it as a mutable bytearray. The OS pages data in and out on demand — no syscalls for reads/writes after the initial mmap. Use cases: (1) large files you need to modify in place, (2) shared memory between processes (mmap.MAP_SHARED), (3) fast random access in large datasets (e.g., indices, database files). Tradeoff: mmap consumes virtual address space (limited on 32-bit), and the file must fit in virtual memory (though not all at once in RAM — paging handles that). For sequential reads of huge files, buffered reads may be faster due to prefetching.
sections.codeBlock:
import mmap

with open("large_data.bin", "r+b") as f:
    # Map the entire file into memory
    with mmap.mmap(f.fileno(), 0) as mm:
        # Access as bytearray — no syscalls!
        print(mm[:10])          # read first 10 bytes
        mm[0:4] = b"DATA"      # modify in place (writes back to file)
        pos = mm.find(b"needle")  # O(n) search in mapped memory
        if pos != -1:
            print(mm[pos:pos+10])

# Shared memory between processes
import mmap, os

mm = mmap.mmap(-1, 1024,  # anonymous, no file
               flags=mmap.MAP_SHARED | mmap.MAP_ANONYMOUS)
pid = os.fork()
if pid == 0:  # child
    mm[:5] = b"hello"
    mm.close()
else:  # parent
    os.waitpid(pid, 0)
    print(mm[:5])  # "hello" — shared memory!
    mm.close()

sections.title: Serialization — JSON & Protocol Buffers
sections.description: JSON is the standard interchange format. Python’s json module maps: dict ↔ object, list ↔ array, str ↔ string, int/float ↔ number, bool ↔ boolean, None ↔ null. Custom serialization via default callable or subclassing json.JSONEncoder. For numeric performance, orjson (Rust-backed) is 3–×5 faster. For large structured data, Protocol Buffers (protobuf) or Apache Arrow give binary encoding with schemas, zero-copy reads, and cross-language support.
sections.codeBlock:
import json

# Basic read/write
with open("data.json") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

with open("data.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)

# Custom serializer for non-standard types
from datetime import datetime

def json_serial(obj):
    if isinstance(obj, datetime):
        return obj.isoformat()
    if isinstance(obj, bytes):
        return obj.hex()
    raise TypeError(f"Type {type(obj)} not serializable")

json.dumps({"ts": datetime.now()}, default=json_serial)

# Performance: use orjson for speed
# import orjson
# orjson.dumps(data)  # bytes output, ~4x faster than json.dumps

# Protocol Buffers example (requires protobuf compiler):
# from my_pb2 import MyMessage
# msg = MyMessage()
# msg.ParseFromString(data)
# with open("out.bin", "wb") as f:
#     f.write(msg.SerializeToString())

sections.description:

  • Python Input and Output — Real Python guide covering all I/O patterns
  • struct — Interpret bytes as packed binary data — Relevant for binary I/O interview questions
